skill.name: bilig-workpaper
skill.description: Use Bilig WorkPaper for spreadsheet-style formulas in Node.js services and AI agent tools without driving Excel or browser spreadsheet UIs.
skill.summary: Run spreadsheet formulas in Node.js services and AI agent tools without Excel or a browser.
skill.author: proompteng

security_audit.summary: All 28 static findings were evaluated and dismissed as false positives. The detected patterns (backtick characters, command substitution syntax, URLs, and text near 'md5'/'sha') all appear in documentation context within SKILL.md. Backticks and $() are listed in a 'Command Safety' section as characters to REJECT, not executed. The URLs are legitimate project documentation references. The skill includes explicit input validation guidance showing security awareness. No malicious intent, prompt injection, or actual dangerous patterns were found.

medium_findings.title: Backtick and command substitution syntax in documentation
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer detected backtick ` and $() patterns as potential shell execution. These appear in the 'Command Safety' section (SKILL.md:38-39) which explicitly warns against building shell commands from user input and lists these characters as values to REJECT. FALSE POSITIVE.

medium_findings.title: npm exec in documentation code block
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged `npm exec` in a JSON config block (SKILL.md:47-61) as external command execution. This is an MCP configuration example using an args array instead of shell string concatenation, which is the recommended safe pattern. FALSE POSITIVE.

medium_findings.title: MCP tool names listed as documentation
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged MCP tool names (list_sheets, read_range, etc.) as backtick execution. These are plain-text function names in a markdown list, not shell commands. FALSE POSITIVE.

medium_findings.title: TypeScript import and method calls
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged TypeScript code (SKILL.md:79-108) containing backtick-like characters. This is a TypeScript code block with imports, method calls, and string literals. No shell execution occurs. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: Documentation reference URLs flagged as hardcoded URLs
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 5 URLs (SKILL.md:125-129) as hardcoded network references. These are documentation links to the project's GitHub Pages site and repository. No data exfiltration or suspicious endpoints detected. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: Non-cryptographic text flagged as cryptographic algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged plain documentation text (SKILL.md:4, SKILL.md:31) as 'weak cryptographic algorithm.' These lines contain standard English text in the YAML frontmatter and usage notes. No cryptographic operations exist in this skill. FALSE POSITIVE.

content.user_title: Run spreadsheet formulas in Node.js without Excel
content.value_statement: This skill eliminates the need for Excel or browser spreadsheet UIs when doing formula-based calculations. It lets AI agents use the Bilig WorkPaper library to compute spreadsheet formulas directly in Node.js services, tests, and MCP tools.
actual_capabilities: Creates spreadsheet workbooks with named sheets and cell references programmatically
Computes spreadsheet formulas without a browser or Excel installed
Reads and writes individual cell values with automatic formula recalculation
Exports workpaper state for persistence and audit trail proof
Validates formulas before applying them to a workbook
Starts a stdio MCP server for AI agent tool integration
limitations: Does not support Excel charts, pivot tables, VBA macros, or COM automation
Limited to formulas supported by the @bilig/headless formula engine
Does not render or display spreadsheets visually
No support for collaborative editing or concurrent multi-user access

use_cases.title: Automate pricing calculations in backend services
use_cases.description: Run formula-driven spreadsheets for pricing, quotes, and payout logic without browser automation or Excel dependencies.

use_cases.title: Validate import logic with spreadsheet formulas
use_cases.description: Use formula checks in test suites to validate import data transformations without driving a spreadsheet UI.

use_cases.title: Let AI agents manipulate spreadsheet data through MCP
use_cases.description: Configure MCP tools so coding agents can read, write, and compute spreadsheet formulas programmatically.

prompt_templates.title: Basic cell formula calculation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a workpaper with an Inputs sheet containing Customers=20 and Average revenue=1200. Add a Summary sheet that computes Revenue as a formula referencing Inputs cells. Calculate the result.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user computing a simple cross-sheet formula

prompt_templates.title: Multi-sheet workbook with cross-sheet references
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a workpaper with three sheets: Rates, Hours, and Total. On the Total sheet, create a formula that multiplies values from the other two sheets. Use named cell references.
prompt_templates.scenario: User managing data across multiple sheets with dependencies

prompt_templates.title: MCP server setup for spreadsheet operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure an MCP server that lets me read and write cells, validate formulas, and export workpaper documents. Use the args array format. Do not build shell commands from user input.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer integrating spreadsheet MCP tools into an AI agent

prompt_templates.title: Programmatic workbook processing with TypeScript
prompt_templates.prompt: Write TypeScript code that loads a workpaper from a file, reads all current cell values, applies a set of updates with formulas, and exports the result with proof of each change.
prompt_templates.scenario: User building automated spreadsheet pipelines in Node.js services

output_examples.input: Calculate revenue as customers times average price.
output_examples.output: Created a workpaper with Inputs (Customers=20, Average revenue=1200) and Summary (Revenue=Inputs!B2*Inputs!B3). The formula computes to 24000. Before values: Customers=20, Avg revenue=1200. After calculation: Revenue=24000. Workpaper state exported for persistence proof.

output_examples.input: Update the customer count from 20 to 32 and show the new revenue.
output_examples.output: Updated cell Inputs!B2 from 20 to 32. Workbook recalculated automatically. Revenue changed from 24000 to 38400 (32 x 1200). Before: Customers=20, Revenue=24000. After: Customers=32, Revenue=38400. Persistence proof exported.

output_examples.input: Validate the formula =Inputs!B2*Inputs!B3 before applying it to the workbook.
output_examples.output: Formula =Inputs!B2*Inputs!B3 validated successfully. The formula references existing cells Inputs!B2 (Customers) and Inputs!B3 (Average revenue). No circular references or invalid cell references detected.

best_practices: Always validate workbook paths and reject special characters when using shell-based commands to prevent injection attacks
Read dependent output cells after each write to verify correct formula recalculation before proceeding
Export and persist workpaper state after each update to maintain a reliable audit trail of changes
anti_patterns: Concatenating user input into shell command strings instead of using MCP args arrays for running npm exec
Assuming exact Excel compatibility for all formulas without testing against the @bilig/headless formula engine
Skipping proof steps and claiming spreadsheet was updated without reading back cell values to confirm

faq.question: What is Bilig WorkPaper?
faq.answer: A Node.js library that computes spreadsheet-style formulas without requiring Excel or a browser.

faq.question: Does this support all Excel formulas?
faq.answer: No. It supports a subset of common spreadsheet formulas. Check the Formula Clinic reference for the supported list.

faq.question: Can I use this with MCP-compatible AI tools?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes configuration for starting a stdio MCP server with the bilig-workpaper-mcp tool.

faq.question: Does this skill execute shell commands?
faq.answer: No. The backtick characters in the skill file appear in a section that warns against shell injection. Only safe MCP args arrays are used.

faq.question: What happens if a formula fails validation?
faq.answer: The skill reports the validation error and does not update the workbook until the formula is corrected.

faq.question: Can I use TypeScript directly instead of the MCP server?
faq.answer: Yes. Import @bilig/headless directly for workbook logic in Node.js services, workers, tests, or API routes.
